groups.title: Essentials
groups.description: Project identity, summary, type, audience, purpose, verified features, repository link, language, depth, and primary reader action.

groups.title: Setup & Usage
groups.description: Technical stack, requirements, installation, package manager, commands, configuration, usage instructions, and examples.

groups.title: Project Presentation
groups.description: Project status, demo and documentation links, badges, media, architecture notes, roadmap, and special requirements.

groups.title: Community & Trust
groups.description: Contribution guidance, issue reporting, security disclosure, license, code of conduct, and acknowledgements.

variables.label: Project name
variables.placeholder: Example: TaskFlow CLI
variables.hint: Enter the public name that should appear at the top of the README.

help.what: Provide the project name before generating the GitHub README.
help.why: The project name cannot be safely inferred and is required for the README title.
help.example: Good example: TaskFlow CLI
help.avoid: Avoid vague placeholders, marketing slogans, unsupported capabilities, fake status claims, or changing the project into a different tool.

variables.label: Short project description
variables.placeholder: Describe what the project does in one or two clear sentences.
variables.hint: Focus on the project’s function and main value rather than marketing slogans.

help.what: Provide the short project description before generating the GitHub README.
help.why: A verified summary is necessary to create an accurate opening description.
help.example: Good example: A command-line tool that helps small teams organize recurring project tasks from plain-text files.
help.avoid: Avoid vague placeholders, marketing slogans, unsupported capabilities, fake status claims, or changing the project into a different tool.

variables.label: Project type
variables.placeholder: Select the closest project type
variables.hint: This changes the README structure, setup instructions, and example format.

help.what: Provide the project type before generating the GitHub README.
help.why: Different software project types require meaningfully different installation, usage, and documentation structures.
help.example: Good example: Command-line tool
help.avoid: Avoid vague placeholders, marketing slogans, unsupported capabilities, fake status claims, or changing the project into a different tool.

variables.label: Primary README audience
variables.placeholder: Choose the main reader
variables.hint: Select the people who should understand and act on the README first.

help.what: Provide the primary readme audience before generating the GitHub README.
help.why: Audience determines the balance between explanation, setup detail, examples, and contribution information.
help.example: Good example: Developers and users
help.avoid: Avoid vague placeholders, marketing slogans, unsupported capabilities, fake status claims, or changing the project into a different tool.

variables.label: Purpose and use case
variables.placeholder: What problem does the project solve, and when should someone use it?
variables.hint: Include the main use case and any important limits on what the project is for.

help.what: Provide the purpose and use case before generating the GitHub README.
help.why: The README needs a concrete reason for the project to exist without inventing positioning.
help.example: Good example: Use it when a team wants simple local task automation without adopting a hosted project-management system.
help.avoid: Avoid vague placeholders, marketing slogans, unsupported capabilities, fake status claims, or changing the project into a different tool.

variables.label: Key features
variables.placeholder: List the most important verified capabilities, one per line.
variables.hint: Include only features that currently exist or clearly label planned features.

help.what: Provide the key features before generating the GitHub README.
help.why: Feature information is project-specific and must be supplied to prevent fabricated capabilities.
help.example: Good example: Parse task files, group tasks by owner, print due-date summaries, and export a Markdown checklist.
help.avoid: Avoid vague placeholders, marketing slogans, unsupported capabilities, fake status claims, or changing the project into a different tool.

variables.label: Repository URL
variables.placeholder: https://github.com/owner/repository
variables.hint: Used only for accurate repository links, clone commands, and references.

help.what: Optional context for the repository url field.
help.why: An exact URL allows correct repository-specific commands and links without guessing the owner or repository name.
help.example: Good example: https://github.com/example/taskflow-cli
help.avoid: Avoid invented, private, shortened, misleading, or unrelated URLs.

variables.label: README language
variables.placeholder: Example: English
variables.hint: Enter the language in which the complete README should be written.

help.what: Optional context for the readme language field.
help.why: The user may need documentation in a language that cannot be inferred from the project details.
help.example: Good example: English
help.avoid: Avoid guessed technical details, unsupported compatibility, fake roadmap promises, secrets, or undocumented behavior.

variables.label: README depth
variables.placeholder: Choose the preferred level of detail
variables.hint: The model will omit unnecessary sections even when a detailed option is selected.

help.what: Optional context for the readme depth field.
help.why: Documentation depth materially changes how much setup, explanation, and example content should be included.
help.example: Good example: Standard
help.avoid: Avoid guessed technical details, unsupported compatibility, fake roadmap promises, secrets, or undocumented behavior.

variables.label: Primary reader action
variables.placeholder: Choose what readers should do next
variables.hint: Select one main action rather than adding several competing requests.

help.what: Optional context for the primary reader action field.
help.why: A README should guide readers toward one proportional next step.
help.example: Good example: Install and try the project
help.avoid: Avoid guessed technical details, unsupported compatibility, fake roadmap promises, secrets, or undocumented behavior.

variables.label: Technology stack
variables.placeholder: List languages, frameworks, libraries, runtime, database, or key services.
variables.hint: Include only confirmed technologies used by the project.

help.what: Optional context for the technology stack field.
help.why: Technical stack helps readers understand requirements and project fit.
help.example: Good example: Node.js, TypeScript, Commander, Vitest
help.avoid: Avoid guessed technical details, unsupported compatibility, fake roadmap promises, secrets, or undocumented behavior.

variables.label: System requirements
variables.placeholder: List runtime versions, operating systems, tools, accounts, SDKs, databases, or services required.
variables.hint: Do not invent minimum versions or optional tools.

help.what: Optional context for the system requirements field.
help.why: Readers need verified prerequisites before attempting setup.
help.example: Good example: Node.js 20 or later; pnpm 9 or later.
help.avoid: Avoid guessed technical details, unsupported compatibility, fake roadmap promises, secrets, or undocumented behavior.

variables.label: Installation method
variables.placeholder: Select installation style
variables.hint: Choose the supported installation path or let the model infer from supplied commands.

help.what: Optional context for the installation method field.
help.why: Installation style determines whether the README shows package, clone, Docker, release, hosted, or mixed instructions.
help.example: Good example: Package manager
help.avoid: Avoid guessed technical details, unsupported compatibility, fake roadmap promises, secrets, or undocumented behavior.

variables.label: Package manager
variables.placeholder: Select package manager
variables.hint: Shown only when package-manager instructions are relevant.

help.what: Optional context for the package manager field.
help.why: Package-manager syntax must match the actual supported workflow.
help.example: Good example: pnpm
help.avoid: Avoid guessed technical details, unsupported compatibility, fake roadmap promises, secrets, or undocumented behavior.

variables.label: Installation commands
variables.placeholder: Paste verified install, clone, build, Docker, or setup commands exactly.
variables.hint: Do not include guessed commands.

help.what: Optional context for the installation commands field.
help.why: Commands are high-risk documentation and must be supplied or clearly marked for verification.
help.example:
Good example: pnpm install
pnpm build
help.avoid: Avoid guessed commands, fake package names, invented environment variables, real secrets, unsupported outputs, or unverified setup steps.

variables.label: Basic usage
variables.placeholder: Explain the first successful use, command, workflow, or API call.
variables.hint: Keep it focused on a real working path.

help.what: Optional context for the basic usage field.
help.why: Usage instructions turn installation into a practical first result.
help.example: Good example: Run `taskflow scan ./tasks` from the repository root.
help.avoid: Avoid guessed commands, fake package names, invented environment variables, real secrets, unsupported outputs, or unverified setup steps.

variables.label: Configuration and environment
variables.placeholder: List real environment variables, config files, defaults, and setup notes.
variables.hint: Do not include real secrets or invented variables.

help.what: Optional context for the configuration and environment field.
help.why: Configuration is often required for a README to be usable.
help.example: Good example: Optional `.taskflowrc` file controls default task directory and output format.
help.avoid: Avoid guessed commands, fake package names, invented environment variables, real secrets, unsupported outputs, or unverified setup steps.

variables.label: Code or command examples
variables.placeholder: Paste verified code, CLI examples, API calls, or expected workflow examples.
variables.hint: Label illustrative output when it is not exact.

help.what: Optional context for the code or command examples field.
help.why: Examples should be accurate enough for readers to adapt safely.
help.example: Good example: taskflow scan ./tasks --owner alex --format markdown
help.avoid: Avoid guessed commands, fake package names, invented environment variables, real secrets, unsupported outputs, or unverified setup steps.

variables.label: Project status
variables.placeholder: Select project status
variables.hint: Choose the honest current status.

help.what: Optional context for the project status field.
help.why: Status affects whether the README needs beta, archive, maintenance, or stability notices.
help.example: Good example: Beta
help.avoid: Avoid guessed technical details, unsupported compatibility, fake roadmap promises, secrets, or undocumented behavior.

variables.label: Demo or live project URL
variables.placeholder: https://www.creatornivo.com/demo
variables.hint: Add only a verified demo, hosted app, preview, or live project URL.

help.what: Optional context for the demo or live project url field.
help.why: Do not invent a demo link.
help.example: Good example: https://www.creatornivo.com/demo
help.avoid: Avoid invented, private, shortened, misleading, or unrelated URLs.

variables.label: Documentation URL
variables.placeholder: https://github.com/owner/repository/docs
variables.hint: Add the official docs, wiki, API reference, or website URL.

help.what: Optional context for the documentation url field.
help.why: Use only real documentation destinations.
help.example: Good example: https://github.com/example/taskflow-cli/docs
help.avoid: Avoid invented, private, shortened, misleading, or unrelated URLs.

variables.label: Badges and build information
variables.placeholder: Paste verified badge image URLs, target URLs, workflow names, package names, or build facts.
variables.hint: Do not invent passing builds, coverage, versions, downloads, stars, or security status.

help.what: Optional context for the badges and build information field.
help.why: Badges are useful only when they reflect real repository signals.
help.example: Good example: Only include confirmed build, test, package, or license badges with exact image and target URLs.
help.avoid: Avoid invented badges, audits, licenses, contributors, media assets, acknowledgements, or trust claims.

variables.label: Screenshots and media
variables.placeholder: List confirmed screenshots, demo GIFs, diagrams, captions, and privacy notes.
variables.hint: Avoid implying media exists when it does not.

help.what: Optional context for the screenshots and media field.
help.why: Visuals can make a README clearer when assets are real and safe to publish.
help.example: Good example: Terminal screenshot showing a sample task summary; hide private project names.
help.avoid: Avoid invented badges, audits, licenses, contributors, media assets, acknowledgements, or trust claims.

variables.label: Architecture notes
variables.placeholder: Describe repository structure, major modules, services, data flow, or design decisions.
variables.hint: Keep planned architecture separate from implemented architecture.

help.what: Optional context for the architecture notes field.
help.why: Architecture context helps technical readers navigate the project.
help.example: Good example: CLI entry point, parser module, reporter module, and file-system adapter.
help.avoid: Avoid guessed technical details, unsupported compatibility, fake roadmap promises, secrets, or undocumented behavior.

variables.label: Roadmap or planned work
variables.placeholder: List planned, experimental, or under-consideration work with status labels.
variables.hint: Do not present planned items as existing features.

help.what: Optional context for the roadmap or planned work field.
help.why: Roadmap notes set expectations without overstating current capability.
help.example: Good example: Planned: JSON export and custom grouping. Not yet implemented.
help.avoid: Avoid guessed technical details, unsupported compatibility, fake roadmap promises, secrets, or undocumented behavior.

variables.label: Additional context
variables.placeholder: Add special requirements, restrictions, facts to include, or claims to avoid.
variables.hint: Use this for information not already covered by another field.

help.what: Optional context for the additional context field.
help.why: A final controlled context field handles project-specific edge cases.
help.example: Good example: Do not claim production readiness or security audit; keep commands exact.
help.avoid: Avoid guessed technical details, unsupported compatibility, fake roadmap promises, secrets, or undocumented behavior.

variables.label: Contribution guidelines
variables.placeholder: Explain who can contribute, how to propose changes, and required checks.
variables.hint: Do not invent contribution acceptance or maintainer availability.

help.what: Optional context for the contribution guidelines field.
help.why: Contribution guidance helps readers participate appropriately.
help.example: Good example: Open an issue before large changes; run tests before submitting a pull request.
help.avoid: Avoid guessed technical details, unsupported compatibility, fake roadmap promises, secrets, or undocumented behavior.

variables.label: Issue tracker URL
variables.placeholder: https://github.com/owner/repository/issues
variables.hint: Add the confirmed place to report bugs or request features.

help.what: Optional context for the issue tracker url field.
help.why: Do not invent support destinations.
help.example: Good example: https://github.com/example/taskflow-cli/issues
help.avoid: Avoid invented, private, shortened, misleading, or unrelated URLs.

variables.label: Security reporting
variables.placeholder: Explain how vulnerabilities should be reported and what should not be posted publicly.
variables.hint: Do not invent private disclosure methods or audits.

help.what: Optional context for the security reporting field.
help.why: Security reporting needs exact, safe instructions.
help.example: Good example: Report vulnerabilities privately through the repository security policy.
help.avoid: Avoid invented badges, audits, licenses, contributors, media assets, acknowledgements, or trust claims.

variables.label: License information
variables.placeholder: Example: MIT, Apache-2.0, Proprietary, or See LICENSE
variables.hint: State the confirmed license or where to find it.

help.what: Optional context for the license information field.
help.why: License information affects whether readers can use, modify, or distribute the project.
help.example: Good example: MIT
help.avoid: Avoid invented badges, audits, licenses, contributors, media assets, acknowledgements, or trust claims.

variables.label: Code of conduct URL
variables.placeholder: https://github.com/owner/repository/blob/main/CODE_OF_CONDUCT.md
variables.hint: Add only a confirmed code-of-conduct URL.

help.what: Optional context for the code of conduct url field.
help.why: Do not imply a policy exists when it does not.
help.example: Good example: https://github.com/example/taskflow-cli/blob/main/CODE_OF_CONDUCT.md
help.avoid: Avoid invented, private, shortened, misleading, or unrelated URLs.

variables.label: Acknowledgements
variables.placeholder: List libraries, contributors, sponsors, inspirations, or third-party assets to acknowledge.
variables.hint: Use only approved attributions.

help.what: Optional context for the acknowledgements field.
help.why: Acknowledgements should be accurate and rights-safe.
help.example: Good example: Thanks to contributors who tested early task-file formats.
help.avoid: Avoid invented badges, audits, licenses, contributors, media assets, acknowledgements, or trust claims.
